tasks.instruction: Przeczytaj ponizszy tekst. Zaznacz, ktore zdania (1.1-1.4) sa zgodne z trescia tekstu (T – True), ktore sa niezgodne (F – False), a ktore zawieraja informacje niepodane w tekscie (NI – No Information). Nastepnie odpowiedz pelnym zdaniem na pytanie 1.5.
tasks.text:
VOLCANOES AND EARTHQUAKES

The surface of the Earth may seem solid and permanent, but in reality it is made up of enormous slabs of rock called tectonic plates that are constantly moving. These plates float on a layer of hot, semi-liquid rock deep beneath the surface. Most of the time, the movement is so slow — just a few centimetres per year — that we cannot feel it. However, when plates collide, pull apart, or slide past each other, the results can be dramatic and sometimes devastating.

Volcanoes typically form at the boundaries where tectonic plates meet. When one plate is pushed beneath another in a process called subduction, rock from the lower plate melts and rises to the surface as magma. If this magma finds a way through cracks in the Earth's crust, a volcanic eruption occurs. Not all eruptions are violent — some volcanoes produce slow-flowing lava that gives people time to evacuate, while others explode with tremendous force, sending ash clouds high into the atmosphere.

Earthquakes are also caused by the movement of tectonic plates. When plates that are locked together suddenly slip, the energy released travels through the Earth as seismic waves. The point underground where the slip occurs is called the focus, and the point on the surface directly above it is the epicentre. Scientists measure earthquakes using the Richter scale, though the more modern moment magnitude scale is now preferred for larger earthquakes.

The region around the Pacific Ocean, known as the Ring of Fire, is home to approximately 75 percent of the world's active volcanoes and is the site of about 90 percent of all earthquakes. Countries such as Japan, Indonesia, Chile, and New Zealand lie along this ring and experience frequent seismic activity. Japan alone records over 1,500 earthquakes per year, though most are too small to be felt.

Scientists cannot yet predict exactly when or where earthquakes will strike, but advances in monitoring technology have improved early warning systems, sometimes giving people a few seconds or minutes to take shelter before the shaking begins.

items.statement: Tectonic plates move too slowly for their movement to have any noticeable effects.
items.explanation: FALSE — While the movement is slow ('just a few centimetres per year'), the text says 'when plates collide, pull apart, or slide past each other, the results can be dramatic and sometimes devastating'.

items.statement: Scientists now generally prefer the moment magnitude scale to the Richter scale for measuring stronger earthquakes.
items.explanation: TRUE — The text states 'the more modern moment magnitude scale is now preferred for larger earthquakes'.

items.statement: Japan has developed the most advanced earthquake early warning system in the world.
items.explanation: NO INFORMATION — The text mentions Japan experiences frequent seismic activity and that early warning systems exist, but does not say which country has the most advanced system.

items.statement: The Ring of Fire is significant because it accounts for the vast majority of global seismic and volcanic activity.
items.explanation: TRUE — The text says the Ring of Fire is 'home to approximately 75 percent of the world's active volcanoes and is the site of about 90 percent of all earthquakes'.

openQuestion.question: Explain the process that leads to a volcanic eruption at a tectonic plate boundary.
openQuestion.modelAnswer: When one tectonic plate is pushed beneath another (subduction), rock from the lower plate melts and rises to the surface as magma. If this magma finds a way through cracks in the Earth's crust, a volcanic eruption occurs.

vocabItems.definition: causing a great deal of damage, destruction, or suffering
the process by which one tectonic plate is pushed beneath another into the Earth's mantle
relating to earthquakes or the vibrations they produce in the ground
the point on the Earth's surface directly above where an earthquake originates underground
to move people away from a dangerous place to somewhere safe
